title: Aha! Moment
oneLiner: The moment users comprehend and appreciate the core value of a product.
definition: The Aha! Moment refers to the precise instant when users initially understand and value the main benefit of a product. It's a crucial point in the user journey where a product's purpose becomes clear, leading to increased engagement and loyalty.
appliesWhen: When introducing a new product or feature
When attempting to increase user engagement
When trying to reduce user churn
do: Ensure your product's core value is easily understandable
Strive to make the Aha! Moment occur as early in the user journey as possible
Continuously monitor and identify the Aha! Moment for your product to improve onboarding and engagement strategies
dont: Overwhelm users with too much information upfront, which could delay the Aha! Moment
Assume that all users will experience the Aha! Moment at the same time
Neglect the user's journey after the Aha! Moment, as continuous engagement is key

title: Attentional Bias
oneLiner: Users naturally gravitate towards information that matches their current mindset.
definition: Attentional Bias refers to the tendency of users to focus on and prioritize information that aligns with their current thoughts, concerns, or beliefs, often unconsciously. This bias can significantly influence how users interact with and perceive a user interface.
appliesWhen: When users are searching for specific information
When users have pre-existing beliefs or concerns
do: Highlight information that is relevant to the user's current context
Use clear and concise information to avoid overwhelming the user
Understand user's mindset and concerns during design process
dont: Overload users with irrelevant information
Ignore user's current concerns in design process
Assume all users will focus on the same information

title: Barnum-Forer Effect
oneLiner: Users often perceive generic information as uniquely applicable to them.
definition: The Barnum-Forer effect is a psychological phenomenon where users perceive vague and general statements as personally meaningful and specifically accurate, even though they apply to a broad range of people. This can influence how users respond to products, services, and content, providing a false sense of personalization.
appliesWhen: When creating user personas or profiles
When crafting marketing messages and user engagement strategies
do: Use this effect to improve user engagement by making content seem more personalized
Consider the Barnum-Forer effect when crafting user profiles and personas
Employ generic but positive statements that users are likely to identify with
dont: Don't overuse this effect as it can lead to user skepticism over time
Avoid making claims that can be easily disproven or are too broad to be meaningful
Don't rely solely on this effect for personalization – it should be complemented with real personalized content

title: Curiosity Gap
oneLiner: Leverage the user's innate desire to fill information gaps to drive engagement and exploration.
definition: The Curiosity Gap is a psychological phenomenon where the need to fill information gaps motivates users to seek additional content. It's a powerful tool that can be used to engage users, encourage exploration, and deepen user interaction with your product or service.
appliesWhen: When designing onboarding experiences to introduce features
When creating marketing or promotional content
When designing content-heavy interfaces like blogs or news sites
do: Create compelling narratives or questions that leave some information out to incite curiosity
Use progressive disclosure to gradually reveal information
Make use of effective CTAs that prompt users to learn more or explore
dont: Avoid leaving out crucial information that could frustrate users
Do not overuse this principle, as it can lead to user fatigue
Don't create false curiosity gaps that mislead or misinform the user

title: Investment Loops
oneLiner: Users who invest efforts into a product are more likely to return.
definition: Investment Loops refers to the tendency of users to return to and engage more deeply with a product or service after they have invested time, effort, or content into it. This investment makes them feel connected to the product, leading to increased usage, loyalty, and overall user retention.
appliesWhen: When designing features that allow users to customize or personalize their experience
When building community-driven platforms where users can contribute content
When designing reward systems that require user input or action
do: Provide opportunities for users to invest their time or effort in meaningful ways
Recognize and reward user contributions to foster a sense of ownership
Make the process of contribution easy and user-friendly
dont: Don't make user investment compulsory for basic usage of the product
Don't neglect user contributions - ensure they feel valued
Don't create a complex process for contribution

title: Progressive Disclosure
oneLiner: Reveal information as users need it, not all at once.
definition: Progressive Disclosure is a design strategy that aims to maintain user focus and reduce clutter by revealing information and options as they become relevant. This prevents overwhelming users with too much information or too many choices at once.
do: Gradually expose advanced features as users become more proficient
Use tooltips or other methods to provide additional information on hover or click
Break long forms into smaller, logically grouped sections
Design interfaces that allow users to drill down into more detailed information
dont: Don't expose users to overwhelming amounts of information at once
Don't hide critical information or features that users need immediately
Don't make users search for information or options they need frequently
Don't use progressive disclosure as an excuse to avoid simplifying complex interfaces

title: Pseudo-Set Framing
oneLiner: Tasks in a collection seem more compelling and worth completing.
definition: Pseudo-Set Framing is a cognitive bias where users feel a greater compulsion to complete tasks or actions when they are presented as part of a collection or set. This is based on the human psychological tendency to complete sets and collections, making users feel more motivated to complete all tasks.
appliesWhen: When designing task lists or progress trackers
When gamifying an application or website
When designing a learning platform with multiple modules
do: Present tasks or actions as part of a collection or set
Provide visual indications of progress through the set
Reward users for completing the set
dont: Overwhelm users with too many tasks in a set
Make tasks within a set too unrelated or disconnected
Ignore the visual design of the set and progress indication

title: Selective Attention
oneLiner: Users naturally filter information, focusing only on elements relevant to their current goals.
definition: Selective Attention refers to the user's ability to focus on specific information while simultaneously ignoring irrelevant information. This principle is based on the cognitive process of selecting and processing certain aspects of information according to their interests, background, and objectives.
appliesWhen: When there is an abundance of information on a page
When users navigate through a complex interface
When users perform tasks that require goal-oriented actions
do: Highlight key information that aligns with user's goals
Minimize irrelevant information or distractions
Use clear and recognizable icons or symbols to guide user's attention
dont: Overload the page with excessive information
Make key actions or information hard to find
Distract users with unnecessary animations or pop-ups

title: Sensory Adaptation
oneLiner: People's perception adjusts to constant, unchanging stimuli over time.
definition: Sensory Adaptation refers to the decrease in sensitivity to a constant stimulus over time. It's the phenomenon where people become less aware of sensory information that remains constant, allowing them to focus on changes in their environment.
do: Emphasize changes or updates in your design to catch attention
Use variation in design elements to prevent sensory adaptation
Incorporate motion or change at regular intervals
Keep critical information dynamic or changing
dont: Don't present important information in a constant, unchanging manner
Avoid overuse of repetitive, static design elements
Don't rely on static elements to attract user attention
Avoid placing critical calls-to-action in areas of constant stimulus

title: Sensory Appeal
oneLiner: Engage multiple senses to forge stronger emotional connections with users.
definition: Sensory Appeal refers to the principle of designing experiences that engage more than one of the user's senses. By appealing to multiple senses, designers can create a more immersive, memorable experience that fosters a deeper emotional connection with the user.
do: Use complementary visual, auditory, or haptic elements.
Consider the emotional response evoked by sensory elements.
Test the sensory components with users to ensure they enhance the experience.
dont: Overwhelm the user with too many sensory stimuli.
Rely only on visual design - consider incorporating sound or haptic feedback.
Ignore accessibility - ensure sensory elements can be perceived by all users.

title: Shaping
oneLiner: Guide users towards desired actions through progressive reinforcement.
definition: Shaping is a UX principle that involves gradually guiding users towards a particular behavior or action through a series of small, successful steps. By reinforcing each step that leads to the desired action, users are encouraged to continue interacting in a way that aligns with the design's intended path.
appliesWhen: When introducing new features or complex functionalities
When guiding the user towards a specific action or behavior
When you want to provide positive reinforcement for user engagement
do: Use small, manageable steps to introduce users to new features
Provide clear feedback to users after each successful step
Reinforce positive user behavior with rewards or positive feedback
dont: Avoid overwhelming users with too much information at once
Don't neglect to provide feedback after each step
Avoid abrupt changes that might confuse the user

title: Spotlight Effect
oneLiner: Users often believe they are under more scrutiny than they actually are.
definition: The Spotlight Effect is a psychological phenomenon where users overestimate the amount of attention others are paying to their actions and appearance. They often feel like they are being closely observed and judged, when in reality, this is rarely the case.
appliesWhen: When users are posting content publicly
When users are creating a profile or avatar
When users interact within a social or community feature
do: Provide reassuring privacy information and controls
Allow users to customize their public visibility
Implement user-friendly error messages and feedback
dont: Create unnecessary stress about public visibility
Over-emphasize the prominence of user actions
Ignore user concerns about privacy and judgment

title: Storytelling Effect
oneLiner: Narratives make information more engaging and memorable than standalone facts.
definition: The Storytelling Effect refers to the phenomenon where information presented in a narrative or story format is more engaging, easier to comprehend, and more memorable than when the same information is presented as isolated facts. This is because stories provide context, making the information more relatable and easier to internalize.
do: Use narratives or stories to present complex information
Provide context to make the information relatable
Use compelling characters or scenarios in your narrative to increase engagement
dont: Overuse the storytelling technique, as it may make the content seem less professional
Make the narrative too complex, as it may distract from the key information
Rely solely on the narrative, neglect important facts or details

title: Von Restorff Effect
oneLiner: Different things stand out and are remembered better
definition: Also known as the isolation effect, items that stand out from their surroundings are more likely to be remembered. Visual distinctiveness helps important elements capture attention and improve recall.
do: Make primary actions visually distinct from secondary ones
Use color, size, or typography to highlight key information
Ensure important buttons stand out from the background
Create visual hierarchy through contrast
dont: Make everything look the same without hierarchy
Overuse highlighting, making everything compete for attention
Hide important actions with low contrast
Use distinctiveness for unimportant elements

title: Affect Heuristic
oneLiner: Emotional states strongly influence judgments and decision-making.
definition: The Affect Heuristic is a mental shortcut that allows people to make decisions and solve problems quickly and efficiently, where current emotional states have a significant impact on decision-making processes. People may rely more on their emotional responses than on a detailed assessment of the situation.
appliesWhen: When users are making quick decisions
When users are in a high-stress environment
When the decision-making scenario is emotionally charged
do: Design interfaces that evoke positive emotions
Consider the user's emotional state when designing decision-making scenarios
Use colors, typography, and images that evoke the desired emotional response
dont: Don't overload the user with information during decision-making
Don't neglect the emotional aspect of your design
Don't make users feel stressed or frustrated during decision-making

title: Anchoring Bias
oneLiner: First impressions heavily influence all subsequent judgments
definition: People rely heavily on the first piece of information encountered (the 'anchor') when making decisions. This initial information serves as a reference point for all subsequent judgments, even when it's irrelevant.
do: Lead with your strongest value proposition
Show higher-value options first in pricing
Create positive first impressions with polished onboarding
Use anchoring strategically in product presentations
dont: Start with negative or confusing experiences
Hide the most attractive options behind less appealing ones
Create poor first impressions that are hard to overcome
Ignore the power of initial positioning

title: Availability Heuristic
oneLiner: People judge likelihood by how easily examples come to mind
definition: People estimate the probability or frequency of an event based on how easily they can recall similar instances. Recent, memorable, or frequently encountered examples seem more likely to occur than they actually are.
do: Provide concrete examples to illustrate abstract concepts
Use familiar analogies to explain new features
Make important information memorable and accessible
Share relevant case studies and success stories
dont: Rely on users to imagine abstract scenarios
Assume users understand risks without examples
Hide important information that should be easily recalled
Use only statistical data without concrete examples

title: Backfire Effect
oneLiner: Contradictory information can reinforce existing beliefs instead of challenging them.
definition: The Backfire Effect refers to the psychological phenomenon where presenting people with evidence that contradicts their existing beliefs actually strengthens those beliefs, rather than changing them. This can be particularly prevalent in situations where beliefs are deeply held or emotionally charged.
appliesWhen: When you're presenting users with new information that conflicts with their existing knowledge
When you're designing content that challenges widely accepted norms or beliefs
When the user's decisions are influenced by their personal beliefs or values
do: Strive to present new information in a non-confrontational manner
Consider the user's existing knowledge and beliefs when designing content
Provide balanced information, allowing users to make their own decisions
dont: Avoid directly challenging a user's beliefs without providing substantial evidence
Don't assume that presenting contradictory evidence will automatically change a user's mind
Do not ignore the potential impact of the Backfire Effect on user decision-making

title: Cashless Effect
oneLiner: Digital payments reduce the psychological discomfort linked to spending money.
definition: The Cashless Effect refers to the phenomenon where users experience less psychological pain or guilt when making purchases through digital payment methods, as compared to using cash. This is due to the abstract nature of digital money, making the loss less tangible and immediate.
do: Provide multiple digital payment options
Emphasize security and trust in digital transactions
Simplify the digital payment process to maximize the cashless effect
dont: Force users to only use tangible forms of payment
Complicate the payment process with unnecessary steps
Overlook the importance of user trust in digital transactions

title: Centre-Stage Effect
oneLiner: Users often prefer options placed in the middle of a set when choices seem equivalent
definition: The Centre-Stage Effect refers to a cognitive bias where users gravitate towards options that are positioned centrally within a set of similar choices. This is especially true when the options appear to have equivalent value or relevance.
appliesWhen: Presenting a row or grid of similar products
Displaying search results with no clear standout
do: Place the most important or recommended options in the middle of the set
Consider the centre-stage effect when designing the layout of choices
Use the centre-stage effect to guide users towards desired actions
dont: Avoid positioning less valuable or less relevant options in the center
Don't overlook the importance of positioning in perceived value
Don't confuse users by placing irrelevant or unrelated choices in the middle

title: Choice Overload
oneLiner: Too many options can paralyze decision-making
definition: When faced with too many choices, people often experience decision paralysis, make poorer choices, or avoid deciding altogether. The paradox of choice suggests that while some choice is good, too much choice can be overwhelming.
do: Curate and limit options to the most relevant ones
Provide smart defaults and recommendations
Use progressive disclosure to reveal options gradually
Group similar options into clear categories
dont: Present hundreds of options without filtering
Make users configure everything from scratch
Offer choices without guidance or recommendations
Create equally prominent options for all features

title: Cognitive Dissonance
oneLiner: Avoid conflicting beliefs or information to prevent mental discomfort for users.
definition: Cognitive dissonance occurs when users encounter conflicting beliefs or information, causing mental discomfort. It's a cognitive bias where the user struggles to reconcile conflicting information, which can lead to confusion, frustration, and reduced satisfaction with the product or service.
appliesWhen: Presenting information or instructions that contradict each other
When user expectations do not align with the product's reality
When the product design or functionality conflicts with the user's pre-existing beliefs or knowledge
do: Ensure information consistency throughout the user journey
Align product design and functionality with user expectations
Clarify ambiguous information or instructions
dont: Provide contradictory information
Design aspects of the product that conflict with common user expectations
Ignore user feedback indicating confusion or frustration

title: Confirmation Bias
oneLiner: People seek information that confirms their existing beliefs
definition: The tendency to search for, interpret, and recall information that confirms pre-existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence. Users approach interfaces with preconceptions about how things should work.
do: Present information objectively and clearly
Test designs with diverse user groups
Challenge your own assumptions during design
Provide multiple perspectives on complex topics
dont: Design based solely on your own preferences
Ignore user feedback that contradicts expectations
Present only information that supports your narrative
Skip user testing with different demographic groups

title: Decision Fatigue
oneLiner: Too many choices exhaust mental energy and impair decision quality.
definition: Decision Fatigue refers to the deteriorating quality of decisions made by an individual, after a long session of decision making. It is now understood as one of the causes of irrational trade-offs in decision making.
appliesWhen: Designing complex interfaces with multiple options
do: Limit the number of choices presented at a time
Implement sensible defaults or 'recommended' options
Arrange choices or steps in a process in order of importance or difficulty
dont: Overwhelm users with too many options at once
Underestimate the cognitive load of decision-making in your interface
Assume that more choices equate to a better user experience

title: Decoy Effect
oneLiner: Make your preferred choice more attractive by introducing an obviously inferior option.
definition: The Decoy Effect is a cognitive bias where people tend to have a change in preference between two options when presented with a third option that is asymmetrically dominated. This third option is the 'decoy' that is intended to make one of the other options more attractive.
appliesWhen: When trying to influence user choices or purchases
When presenting multiple pricing or package options
do: Introduce an option that is clearly inferior to make your preferred choice stand out
Ensure the decoy is less attractive but comparable to the most expensive or comprehensive option
Use this effect carefully and ethically to guide users towards beneficial decisions
dont: Overuse this effect, as it can lead to mistrust if users feel manipulated
Use this effect to mislead users into harmful or non-beneficial decisions
Rely solely on this effect; always maintain user value and service quality

title: Default Bias
oneLiner: Users tend to stick with the default rather than make a change.
definition: Default Bias is a cognitive bias where users are more likely to accept the pre-selected options, rather than making active choices. This bias is driven by effort avoidance and status quo bias, making defaults significantly influential in user decisions.
appliesWhen: When designing form fields with pre-selected options
When designing user onboarding with recommended settings
do: Make the default option beneficial for most users
Ensure the default options align with user goals
Use default options to simplify complex choices
dont: Don’t make critical decisions solely dependent on default selections
Don't use defaults to manipulate or mislead users
Don't set default options that could lead to negative outcomes if not changed

title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
oneLiner: People with low ability overestimate their competence
definition: Individuals with limited knowledge or competence in a domain overestimate their own knowledge or competence in that domain. This affects how users perceive their skill level and their willingness to seek help or follow guidance.
do: Provide progressive learning paths for complex features
Offer both beginner and advanced modes
Make help and tutorials easily accessible
Guide users through complex tasks step-by-step
dont: Assume all users know their actual skill level
Hide help systems or make them hard to find
Design only for expert users
Skip educational content for complex features

title: Empathy Gap
oneLiner: Don't underestimate the role of emotions in user decisions.
definition: The Empathy Gap refers to the common tendency to underestimate the influence of emotional states on user decision-making processes. It's the miscalculation that occurs when designers fail to account for the emotional factors that can significantly impact how users engage with a product.
appliesWhen: When designing user journeys and workflows
When creating interactive elements and micro-interactions
do: Consider the emotional state of the user when interacting with your product
Design for emotional scenarios, not just functional ones
Use personas and user research to understand the emotional context of your users
dont: Don't assume users will always make logical, rational decisions
Don't neglect the importance of emotional design
Don't design in a vacuum, devoid of user context and emotions

title: Endowment Effect
oneLiner: Users value what they own more than what they don't.
definition: The Endowment Effect refers to a cognitive bias where users assign greater value to items they perceive as belonging to them. It's a psychological phenomenon that suggests ownership increases perceived value.
appliesWhen: When designing customizable features or products
When seeking user commitment or loyalty
When aiming to encourage user purchases
do: Allow users to personalize or customize their experiences
Highlight the value of products or features users have chosen or purchased
Use the Endowment Effect to encourage emotional attachment and user loyalty
dont: Don't underestimate the value users place on their choices or possessions
Don't remove or change user-customized settings or features without clear communication and consent
Don't neglect opportunities to amplify the Endowment Effect through personalization

title: False Consensus Effect
oneLiner: Users tend to overestimate the number of people who share their opinions and preferences.
definition: The False Consensus Effect is a cognitive bias where individuals assume that their own personal preferences, beliefs, values, and habits are 'normal' and that others also think the same way that they do. This bias can lead to a misconception of user needs by designers and developers.
appliesWhen: Designing interface elements based on personal preference
do: Involve a diverse range of users in testing and feedback
Avoid design decisions based solely on personal preference or assumption
Validate assumptions about user behavior with data
dont: Don't design based solely on your own preferences
Don't assume user needs without research
Don't ignore feedback that contradicts your assumptions

title: Framing Principle
oneLiner: The way information is presented significantly affects how users perceive and interpret it.
definition: The Framing Principle suggests that the context, layout, and presentation of information play a crucial role in influencing a user's interpretation and decision-making process. The framing effect can lead to different perceptions and responses based on how the same information is framed or presented.
appliesWhen: Designing information presentation in an interface
Constructing messages for user prompts and notifications
do: Present information in multiple frames to cater to diverse user perceptions
Frame information in a positive context to encourage user engagement
Use clear and concise language to avoid misinterpretations
dont: Avoid presenting information in a negative or confusing frame
Do not overload users with too many frames at once
Do not use misleading frames that could deceive the user

title: Fresh Start Effect
oneLiner: New beginnings and temporal landmarks boost motivation for change.
definition: The Fresh Start Effect is a cognitive bias where individuals show increased willingness to engage in aspirational behavior following temporal landmarks (e.g., the start of a new week, month, year, or significant event). These 'fresh starts' psychologically separate individuals from past failures, enhancing their motivation to achieve goals.
appliesWhen: When designing goal setting or habit tracking features in an app
When creating onboarding experiences for new users
When designing a product or service that promotes behavioral change
do: Leverage significant dates or events as 'fresh start' opportunities
Promote aspirational goals or tasks at the beginning of new time periods
Use reminders or notifications to reinforce the 'fresh start'
dont: Ignore the psychological impact of new beginnings
Overwhelm users with too many goals or tasks at once
Forget to provide positive feedback or reinforcement when users make progress

title: Goal Gradient Effect
oneLiner: Users are more motivated as they see progress towards a goal.
definition: The Goal Gradient Effect describes the tendency of users to increase efforts and engagement as they perceive they are nearing the completion of a goal or objective. It leverages the human desire to complete tasks and achieve goals, making users more committed as they get closer to the finish line.
appliesWhen: When incentivizing user engagement or progression
do: Use progress bars or indicators to visually show users their advancement towards a goal
Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps to make progress more apparent
Provide instant feedback on user actions to reinforce the sense of progress
dont: Avoid vague or unclear progression indicators
Don't make the goal too easy or too hard to achieve
Don't neglect to reward or acknowledge user's progress

title: Halo Effect
oneLiner: First impressions count, as they influence perception of subsequent unrelated features.
definition: The Halo Effect refers to the cognitive bias where an initial positive impression of a product, service, or individual influences how users perceive other aspects of it, often unrelated to the initial impression. It emphasizes how positive user experiences in one area can spill over and enhance perception in others.
appliesWhen: When a product or service is being introduced to a user for the first time
When redesigning or updating an existing product or service
When trying to improve the overall perception of a product or service
do: Ensure the first interaction or experience with your product or service is positive
Highlight the most impressive features or aspects of your product early on
Maintain consistent quality across all aspects of your product to reinforce the initial positive impression
dont: Ignore the importance of first impressions
Neglect unrelated aspects of your product assuming they won't affect the overall perception
Assume that a positive initial impression will automatically lead to long-term user satisfaction

title: Hindsight Bias
oneLiner: Avoid designing on the assumption that users knew the outcome all along
definition: Hindsight bias refers to the phenomenon where people overestimate their ability to have predicted an outcome after they've learned the results. In UX, this can lead to the false assumption that users should have known how to use a feature or avoid an error.
appliesWhen: Designing feedback mechanisms after user actions
do: Present clear steps or instructions before users perform actions
Provide adequate feedback during and after user actions
Test your design assumptions with actual users
dont: Assume users can predict the outcome of their actions
Make conclusions about user behaviors based solely on post-outcome analysis
Design complex tasks without guidance or feedback

title: Hyperbolic Discounting
oneLiner: Users tend to overvalue immediate benefits over larger future rewards.
definition: Hyperbolic Discounting is a cognitive bias where users exhibit the tendency to choose smaller, immediate rewards over larger, delayed ones. This can significantly impact user decision-making and behavior within a digital environment.
appliesWhen: When designing reward systems or incentives
When planning for long term user engagement
When designing a purchase or subscription model
do: Offer immediate rewards or benefits to drive immediate action
Use time-limited offers or incentives to encourage quick decisions
Highlight the immediate benefits of a product or service
dont: Overly rely on future benefits to motivate user behavior
Assume users will make rational choices that maximize long-term value
Neglect immediate user satisfaction in favor of long-term gains

title: IKEA Effect
oneLiner: Users value products more if they have contributed to their creation or customization.
definition: The IKEA Effect refers to a cognitive bias where users place a disproportionately high value on products they partially created or customized. This effect illustrates a stronger sense of ownership and satisfaction when users are involved in the creation process.
appliesWhen: When the product allows customization options
When users are involved in the creation process
When the product requires assembly or user contribution
do: Provide customization options whenever possible
Engage users in the product creation process
Create DIY or assembly-required versions of the product
dont: Ignore the importance of user involvement in the product's setup or customization
Over-complicate the assembly or customization process
Underestimate the impact of user satisfaction from creating or customizing a product

title: Labor Illusion
oneLiner: Users perceive more value when they see the work put into a product or service.
definition: Labor Illusion is a cognitive bias where users perceive a product or service to be more valuable when they can observe or are made aware of the effort, time, and work that went into its creation or delivery. It is based on the psychological principle that people value things more when they can see the labor invested.
appliesWhen: When users are waiting for a service to be delivered
When the process behind a product or service is complex and unseen
When there's a need to justify the cost or value of a product or service
do: Provide visual feedback or indicators of the work being done
Communicate the complexity and effort behind your product or service
Show progress bars or loading indicators for time-intensive tasks
dont: Hide the complexity or work involved in providing a service
Make processes completely transparent without showing any effort
Ignore the opportunity to illustrate the value through the display of labor

title: Law of the Instrument
oneLiner: Overuse of a familiar tool can stifle creative problem-solving.
definition: The Law of the Instrument, also known as Maslow's Hammer, suggests that people tend to over-rely on familiar tools or methods, even when they are not the most effective. This can limit creative problem-solving and prevent exploration of more efficient solutions.
appliesWhen: When a user has a preferred tool or method they use constantly
When there are multiple solutions to a problem but the user defaults to a known method
When a user remains within their comfort zone instead of exploring new options
do: Encourage exploration of different tools or methods
Design systems to expose users to a variety of solutions
Provide clear incentives for users to try out new tools
dont: Do not limit users to a single method or tool
Avoid designing systems that solely reinforce familiar methods
Do not discourage experimentation or exploration

title: Loss Aversion
oneLiner: People fear losing something more than gaining equivalent value
definition: The psychological principle that people feel the pain of losing something twice as strongly as the pleasure of gaining the same thing. Users are more motivated to avoid losses than to acquire gains of equal value.
do: Frame benefits in terms of what users will lose without them
Provide clear warnings before destructive actions
Offer to save or backup important user data
Emphasize existing investments when preventing churn
dont: Delete user data without clear warnings
Remove features without explaining alternatives
Make it easy to accidentally lose progress or content
Focus only on gains without acknowledging potential losses

title: Noble Edge Effect
oneLiner: Users are more attracted to brands that showcase social responsibility and ethical values.
definition: The Noble Edge Effect refers to the inclination of users or consumers towards brands and products that exhibit social responsibility and uphold ethical values. It is a cognitive bias where users form a positive perception and preference for brands that demonstrate a commitment to societal or environmental issues.
appliesWhen: When building your brand identity and values
When communicating your brand's mission and achievements
When designing marketing and advertising campaigns
do: Highlight your brand's commitment to social responsibility in your messaging
Incorporate social responsibility and ethical values into your brand's identity
Engage in initiatives that demonstrate your commitment to societal or environmental issues
dont: Don't make false or exaggerated claims about your brand's social or environmental impact
Don't ignore or sideline your brand's social responsibility in your messaging
Don't engage in activities that contradict your brand's proclaimed social responsibility and ethical values

title: Observer-Expectancy Effect
oneLiner: Researcher's expectations can subconsciously shape participant behavior.
definition: The Observer-Expectancy Effect is a cognitive bias where a researcher's expectations unconsciously influence the behavior of the subjects in a study or testing scenario. This could lead to distorted results that may not reflect the true user behavior or preferences.
do: Ensure the neutrality of the researcher
Use blind testing methods where possible
Be aware of potential biases and constantly question your assumptions
dont: Don't allow the researcher to lead the participant
Don't design tests that are overly influenced by the researcher's hypothesis
Don't ignore potential biases in your research methodology

title: Planning Fallacy
oneLiner: People underestimate the time, resources, and effort needed to complete tasks.
definition: The planning fallacy refers to the tendency of individuals to underestimate the time, resources, and effort required to complete a task, even when similar tasks have taken longer in the past. This cognitive bias often leads to unrealistic project timelines and overworked teams.
appliesWhen: When allocating resources for a task
do: Consider historical data or past experiences when estimating
Allow for extra time and resources as a buffer
Regularly update estimates as tasks progress
dont: Ignore past experiences or data
Set fixed deadlines without considering potential delays
Overload your team based on underestimated timelines

title: Priming
oneLiner: Priming leverages users' past experiences to influence their interaction with a design.
definition: Priming, a cognitive bias in UX design, is the principle where a user's exposure to one stimulus influences their response to a subsequent stimulus. This is often leveraged in UX design to guide user behavior and interactions based on their past experiences or pre-existing knowledge.
do: Use familiar icons and symbols to guide user behavior and make the interface intuitive.
Employ color, size, and placement strategically to direct user attention and prompt desired actions.
Capitalise on users' prior experiences to make interfaces feel familiar and intuitive.
dont: Avoid confusing users with inconsistent use of symbols, colors, or design elements.
Never overlook the significant impact of a user's past experiences on their interactions with your design.
Don't rely solely on priming; ensure usability and accessibility are prioritized in all aspects of your design.

title: Reactance Principle
oneLiner: Users resist when they feel their freedom of choice is threatened
definition: Reactance occurs when a user feels their freedom to choose is being restricted or limited. This often leads to resistance or outright rejection of the presented options, even if they are beneficial to the user.
appliesWhen: When presenting options in a decision-making process
When the system attempts to guide user behavior
When the platform limits user choices or customization
do: Provide users with a variety of options to choose from
Allow users to customize their experience
Show respect for user autonomy in design
dont: Force users into a specific path or decision
Limit customization options unnecessarily
Make users feel manipulated or controlled

title: Self-serving Bias
oneLiner: Users tend to credit their successes to their abilities and blame their failures on external factors.
definition: Self-serving bias is a cognitive bias that causes individuals to attribute positive outcomes to their own actions and negative outcomes to external factors. This bias affects how users perceive and interact with interfaces, often leading to misinterpretations of their own abilities and the product's functionality.
appliesWhen: When a user successfully completes a task or action within the interface
When a user fails to accomplish a goal using the product
When the interface provides feedback on user performance or actions
do: Design clear and concise feedback mechanisms to guide users about correct and incorrect actions
Provide support and guided assistance in case of errors or difficulties
Ensure user instructions and interface design are intuitive and easy to understand
dont: Don't blame users for errors or difficulties. Instead, use these instances as opportunities for improvement
Don't make the interface overly complex or difficult to navigate
Don't withhold feedback from users, especially during complex tasks

title: Spark Effect
oneLiner: Users tend to complete tasks more swiftly when perceived effort is minimal.
definition: The Spark Effect is a cognitive bias where users are more motivated to complete tasks if they perceive the required effort to be minimal. This principle is based on the psychology of human decision-making and motivation, where tasks are more likely to be undertaken and completed if they appear easy or quick to do.
do: Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
Provide progress indicators to show users how much they've completed and what's left
Use clear and concise instructions to minimize cognitive load
dont: Overload users with unnecessary information or steps
Make tasks seem longer or more complicated than they actually are
Ignore the importance of user perception in task completion

title: Sunk Cost Fallacy
oneLiner: People continue bad decisions because they've already invested
definition: The tendency to continue investing in a losing proposition because of previously invested resources (time, money, or effort). Users may stick with poor solutions simply because they've already put effort into them.
do: Acknowledge users' previous investments when proposing changes
Provide migration tools and clear transition paths
Explain future benefits that outweigh past investments
Make new solutions clearly superior to old ones
dont: Ignore the effort users have put into current workflows
Make switching costly or complicated
Dismiss existing solutions without showing better alternatives
Force changes without explaining long-term benefits

title: Survivorship Bias
oneLiner: Don't just focus on successful outcomes, failures are equally informative.
definition: Survivorship bias is a logical error of concentrating on the people or things that made it past some selection process and overlooking those that did not, typically because of their lack of visibility. In UX design, this could mean only considering successful user interactions and ignoring instances where users struggled or failed to complete tasks.
appliesWhen: When evaluating the usability of a product or feature
do: Consider both successful and unsuccessful user interactions when making design decisions
Analyze why certain parts of a design failed and how they can be improved
Use both positive and negative data to inform future design iterations
dont: Don't ignore or dismiss failures as outliers or irrelevant
Don't base design decisions solely on successful outcomes
Don't overlook the importance of learning from unsuccessful designs or features

title: Unit Bias
oneLiner: Users perceive single servings or portions as the natural and appropriate amount
definition: Unit bias is a cognitive bias in which individuals believe that a single unit or portion of any given item is the correct and satisfactory amount. This principle suggests that users feel more satisfied and comfortable when they consume, use, or interact with single units or portions.
appliesWhen: Setting default values in forms or interfaces
Creating meal plans or subscription boxes
do: Where possible, offer products or services in single units
Set default options to single units
Consider user satisfaction and perceived value in portioning
dont: Overwhelm users by offering only bulk options
Assume users always want to maximize quantity over satisfaction
Disregard unit bias when pricing and packaging products

title: Variable Reward Principle
oneLiner: Unpredictable rewards spark stronger user motivation than consistent ones.
definition: The Variable Reward Principle is based on the concept that unpredictable positive outcomes stimulate higher engagement and motivation than predictable rewards. It is rooted in the psychological concept of operant conditioning, where an individual's behavior is modified by its consequences.
appliesWhen: When designing reward or loyalty programs
do: Incorporate elements of surprise and unpredictability in the reward system
Ensure the rewards are genuinely beneficial or desirable to the user
Maintain a balance between predictable and unpredictable rewards to ensure user engagement and satisfaction
dont: Don't make the rewards too unpredictable that users feel cheated or frustrated
Don't completely eliminate predictable rewards as they provide a sense of stability and trust
Don't overuse this principle, as it can lead to addiction or habituation

title: Chunking
oneLiner: Divide and conquer information overload by grouping related data into smaller, digestible chunks.
definition: Chunking is a UX principle that advocates for the structuring of information into smaller, related sets. This makes it easier for users to process, understand, and remember the information.
appliesWhen: Presenting detailed instructions or complex information to users.
Designing forms or data collection interfaces.
Organizing content on a webpage, especially when it's heavy with text.
do: Group related information together to make it easier for users to process.
Limit the number of chunks to between five and nine items, as this is the range most people can comfortably remember.
Use visual cues, like borders or white space, to clearly distinguish between different chunks of information.
dont: Overwhelm users by presenting all information at once without any segmentation.
Create chunks that are too large; if a chunk has more than nine items, consider breaking it down further.
Mix unrelated information in the same chunk; this can confuse users and make it harder for them to remember.

title: Cognitive Load
oneLiner: Minimize mental effort to enhance user engagement and comprehension.
definition: Cognitive Load refers to the total amount of mental effort being used in the working memory. In the context of user interface design, cognitive load is the amount of brain power required to use the product. The less cognitive load, the easier the product is to use.
appliesWhen: When a lot of information needs to be processed
When the user needs to learn a new system
do: Make interfaces simple and intuitive
Group related items together to benefit from chunking
Use progressive disclosure to hide complexity
dont: Overload the user with too much information at once
Make the user remember information from one part of the interface to another
Use inconsistent UI elements which require learning new interactions

title: Method of Loci
oneLiner: Improve user recall and learning retention by associating information with specific locations.
definition: This principle is based on the psychological technique of associating information with specific physical locations or paths, also known as memory palaces, to improve recall and learning retention. In digital UX, this can translate to the spatial arrangement of information or visual elements on a screen or within a user interface.
appliesWhen: When creating complex interfaces with a large amount of information
When user recall of specific information is vital
do: Organize information in a spatially meaningful manner
Use consistent spatial locations for repeated elements or information
Associate key information with distinct visual or spatial markers
dont: Randomly change the location of elements or information
Overcrowd the interface making it hard for users to associate information with locations
Ignore the importance of spatial consistency in your design

title: Miller's Rule
oneLiner: People can only hold 7±2 items in working memory
definition: The average person can hold about 7 (plus or minus 2) items in their working memory at once. This applies to navigation menus, form fields, and any interface requiring users to remember multiple pieces of information.
do: Limit main navigation to 5-9 items maximum
Break long forms into smaller, logical sections
Use chunking to group related information
Provide clear progress indicators for multi-step processes
dont: Create navigation menus with more than 9 top-level items
Present long lists without categorization
Design forms with too many fields on one screen
Expect users to remember complex sequences

title: Negativity Bias
oneLiner: Users tend to remember and give more weight to negative experiences.
definition: Negativity bias is a cognitive bias where users give more weight to negative experiences or information compared to positive ones. This bias can heavily influence the user's overall perception of a product or service, often leading to a more critical evaluation.
appliesWhen: When users interact with complex features
When users encounter errors or obstacles
When users receive feedback on their actions
do: Design clear and easy-to-use interfaces to minimize user frustration
Provide helpful error messages to guide users smoothly through issues
Ensure positive user experiences outweigh any potential negative ones
dont: Ignore user complaints or negative feedback
Underestimate the impact of a single negative experience
Make it difficult for users to recover from errors

title: Peak-End Rule
oneLiner: People judge experiences by their peak moment and how they end
definition: Users remember an experience largely based on how they felt at its peak (most intense point) and at its end, rather than the total sum or average of every moment. The duration has little effect on the memory.
do: Design memorable positive peak moments
End experiences on a high note with celebration or success
Focus on making critical moments delightful
Provide satisfying completion states
dont: End experiences with frustration or confusion
Make the most intensive moments negative
Ignore the emotional journey of user interactions
Focus only on efficiency without considering feelings

title: Recognition over Recall
oneLiner: It's easier to recognize things than to remember them
definition: Human memory is better at recognizing previously encountered items than recalling them from scratch. Users find it easier to choose from a list of options than to generate answers from memory.
do: Provide dropdown menus instead of requiring typed input
Show recently used items or suggestions
Use autocomplete and search suggestions
Display available options clearly
dont: Force users to remember exact commands or syntax
Hide available options behind unclear labels
Require users to recall precise formatting
Make users guess what inputs are acceptable

title: Serial Position Effect
oneLiner: Users remember first and last items best
definition: In a list of items, people tend to remember the first few (primacy effect) and last few (recency effect) items best, while items in the middle are more likely to be forgotten or overlooked.
do: Place the most important items at the beginning or end of lists
Position key navigation items at the start or end of menus
End forms with clear call-to-action buttons
Start content with compelling hooks
dont: Bury important items in the middle of long lists
Place critical navigation in the center of menus
Hide important information in the middle of content
Make all list items look equally important

title: Spacing Effect
oneLiner: Spread learning and practice sessions over time for better retention and understanding.
definition: The Spacing Effect is a cognitive bias that suggests learning and memory retention can be improved when practice sessions are distributed over time rather than crammed into a single session. This principle highlights the importance of repetition and rest in learning processes.
do: Incorporate repetition and rest periods in learning or training modules
Space out reminders or prompts over time
Distribute onboarding steps over user's initial interaction period
dont: Cram all information or training into a single session
Overwhelm users with too much information at once
Expect users to remember complex tasks after a single exposure

title: Zeigarnik Effect
oneLiner: People remember interrupted tasks better than completed ones
definition: Incomplete or interrupted tasks are better remembered than completed tasks. This creates mental tension that keeps the task active in memory until it's finished, which can be leveraged to increase engagement.
do: Show progress indicators to create completion desire
Use progress bars in multi-step processes
Highlight partially completed profiles or forms
Create natural stopping points in long content
dont: Leave users without clear next steps
Create overly complex multi-step processes
Make progress indicators misleading or inaccurate
Force completion of unnecessary steps

title: Authority Bias
oneLiner: Users tend to trust and follow advice from perceived experts more than others.
definition: Authority Bias is a psychological principle where users show a tendency to place an undue amount of value on opinions, recommendations, or information from perceived authorities or experts. This bias can disproportionately influence decision-making and user behavior.
appliesWhen: When users need guidance or advice
When users are uncertain about their decisions
When the product or service is complex or technical
do: Highlight expert opinions or endorsements
Leverage testimonials from recognized authorities in your field
Use credentials, qualifications, and other authority indicators in content
dont: Misrepresent the expertise or authority of a recommendation source
Overuse authority bias, as it can lead to skepticism
Rely solely on authority figures, ignoring user's personal needs and preferences

title: Commitment and Consistency Principle
oneLiner: Users prefer to stay consistent with their previous actions and commitments.
definition: This principle is based on the human tendency to align present actions with past behaviors and commitments. Users are more likely to continue a started action, remain on a chosen path, and interact consistently with an interface that matches their previous actions or stated beliefs.
appliesWhen: When users make selections or choices
When users are required to complete a multi-step process
When users are asked to commit to a decision
do: Provide feedback that reinforces users' decisions
Design flows that build on users' previous actions
Use default settings that align with typical user choices
dont: Do not change interface drastically without user's awareness
Do not ask users to make contradictory decisions
Do not introduce unexpected actions that conflict with users' previous decisions

title: Principle of Reciprocity
oneLiner: Users are more likely to return favors and respond positively to gifts or helpful gestures.
definition: The Principle of Reciprocity refers to the human tendency to want to return favors or reciprocate positive actions. In UX design, this principle can be applied to create a sense of obligation in users, encouraging them to engage more with the product or service.
appliesWhen: When designing user onboarding or tutorials
When seeking user feedback or survey completion
When promoting premium features or upselling
do: Offer users something of value before asking for something in return
Use personalized and helpful gestures to make users feel special
Create opportunities for users to reciprocate
dont: Don't make the reciprocity feel forced or manipulative
Don't overuse this principle as it may lose its effect
Don't make the offering irrelevant or useless

title: Scarcity Principle
oneLiner: Users value and desire items more when they are perceived as rare or limited.
definition: The scarcity principle refers to the cognitive bias where users perceive items that are rare or in limited supply as more valuable or desirable. This phenomenon can affect their decision-making process, causing them to act more quickly in fear of missing out.
appliesWhen: Users are shopping or making purchase decisions
Users are booking services with limited availability such as hotels, flights, or events
Users are deciding whether to sign up for a limited-time offer or promotion
do: Highlight the limited availability or rarity of a product or service to increase its perceived value
Use countdown timers or stock levels to create a sense of urgency
Offer exclusive or limited-time promotions to encourage immediate action
dont: Don't overuse scarcity tactics as it can lead to user fatigue and reduce their effectiveness
Don't create artificial scarcity that can lead to user distrust if discovered
Don't withhold essential product information in the name of creating scarcity

title: Social Desirability Bias
oneLiner: Users may alter their responses to present themselves in a more favorable light.
definition: Social Desirability Bias refers to the propensity of individuals to respond to surveys or questions in a way that is socially acceptable or desirable, even if it doesn't reflect their true feelings, behavior, or attitudes. This can lead to skewed results, misinterpretation of data, and inaccurate insights.
appliesWhen: When conducting user interviews or usability tests
do: Use anonymous surveys to reduce the impact of social desirability bias
Ask questions in a non-judgmental way
Use indirect questioning techniques to get at sensitive or potentially embarrassing information
dont: Don't rely solely on self-reported data for important decisions
Don't phrase questions in a way that implies a 'right' or 'wrong' answer
Don't overlook the potential for this bias when analyzing and interpreting data

title: Social Proof
oneLiner: Users often rely on the decisions and behaviors of others to inform their own actions.
definition: Social Proof is a psychological and social phenomenon where individuals observe the actions of others and then make decisions based on these observations. It's based on the assumption that surrounding people possess more knowledge about the situation.
appliesWhen: When users are unsure about a decision
When users are in a new or unfamiliar environment
When there's a large number of users or community involved
do: Show user testimonials, reviews, or endorsements
Highlight the number of users who have chosen a particular option or product
Show social media shares or likes to reinforce popularity
dont: Fabricate or manipulate social proof indicators
Overwhelm users with too many social proof elements
Rely solely on social proof as your product's value proposition

title: Consistency & Standards
oneLiner: Similar things should look and behave the same way
definition: Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Consistency within your product and adherence to platform conventions reduces cognitive load and improves usability.
do: Use consistent terminology throughout your product
Apply the same interaction patterns for similar actions
Follow platform-specific design guidelines
Maintain visual consistency across all screens
dont: Use different words for the same concept
Change interaction patterns arbitrarily between screens
Ignore platform conventions without good reason
Create inconsistent visual treatments for similar elements

title: Curse of Knowledge
oneLiner: Avoid assuming users know as much about your product as you do.
definition: The Curse of Knowledge is a cognitive bias where designers, due to their own deep knowledge of a product, overestimate the level of understanding or knowledge of their users. This can lead to confusion and usability issues as users may not understand the product as intuitively.
do: Conduct user testing to understand users' knowledge level
Involve users in the design process
Create intuitive, user-friendly designs
dont: Assume users have the same understanding as you
Overcomplicate the design
Neglect user feedback or testing

title: Delighters Principle
oneLiner: Exceed user expectations with unexpected positive experiences for memorable moments.
definition: The Delighters Principle suggests that providing users with unexpected and positive interactions can lead to memorable experiences, thereby exceeding their expectations and increasing satisfaction. This principle is highly influential in creating a positive emotional response, reinforcing user engagement and increasing brand loyalty.
appliesWhen: When designing micro-interactions within the user interface
do: Surprise users with meaningful and positive interactions
Create engaging, fun, and unique experiences
Incorporate positive feedback mechanisms in your design
dont: Overwhelm users with too many unexpected interactions
Neglect user's main goals in pursuit of delight
Create unnecessary complexity in an attempt to surprise and delight

title: Discoverability
oneLiner: Design for users to swiftly find and access key features in your interface
definition: Discoverability refers to how easily a user can identify and utilize important functions within a digital interface. The easier it is for users to find and use key functionalities, the better the user experience.
appliesWhen: Designing a menu or navigation system
Incorporating new features in an application
Building a complex interface with multiple functionalities
do: Prioritize key features to be readily visible
Use clear labels and icons for functions
Provide tool tips and help options to guide users
dont: Hide crucial features under complex menu structures
Rely only on users' intuition to discover features
Overload the interface with too many visible options

title: Error Prevention
oneLiner: Prevent problems before they occur
definition: Good design prevents users from making errors in the first place. This involves eliminating error-prone conditions, asking for confirmation of destructive actions, and providing constraints that guide users toward correct choices.
do: Validate inputs in real-time as users type
Ask for confirmation before destructive actions
Provide clear constraints and formatting examples
Disable buttons that would cause errors
dont: Wait until form submission to show validation errors
Allow destructive actions without warning
Accept clearly invalid input without guidance
Make it easy to accidentally trigger critical actions

title: Expectations Bias
oneLiner: Users interpret and engage with designs based on their pre-existing beliefs and assumptions.
definition: Expectations Bias refers to the influence of users' preconceived notions on their perception and interaction with a design. These pre-existing beliefs or assumptions, derived from past experiences or cultural norms, can significantly shape the users' experience and their overall evaluation of the design.
do: Investigate and understand your users' expectations before designing
Design familiar patterns to meet users' preconceived notions
Conduct usability testing to identify if the design matches user expectations
dont: Ignore the importance of cultural and individual differences in shaping expectations
Assume all users have the same expectations
Design complex interfaces without considering user expectations

title: External Trigger
oneLiner: Use environmental cues to prompt users towards specific actions.
definition: External Triggers are cues in the environment that inform a user’s action within a user interface. They serve as a guide, directing users to perform specific tasks or behaviors, thereby enhancing the user experience.
appliesWhen: Guiding users through a new feature or process
do: Ensure external triggers are clear and intuitive
Place triggers strategically to guide user's attention
Use triggers to simplify complex processes
dont: Overload the interface with too many triggers
Use vague or ambiguous triggers
Rely only on triggers without providing necessary information or support

title: Familiarity Bias
oneLiner: Users gravitate towards interfaces that mirror their past experiences.
definition: Familiarity Bias is a cognitive bias where users prefer interfaces and interactions that align with their previous experiences. This principle underlines the importance of using familiar design patterns and interaction styles to minimize the learning curve and enhance usability.
appliesWhen: Adding new features to an existing application
do: Utilize common design patterns and conventions
Maintain consistency in your design
Consider the user’s previous experiences and preferences during the design process
dont: Overcomplicate the user interface with unfamiliar elements
Change the interface drastically without a clear need
Ignore well-established design patterns without a strong rationale

title: Feedback Loop Principle
oneLiner: Immediate system responses clarify user action outcomes
definition: The Feedback Loop Principle refers to the idea that users should receive immediate responses or feedback from the system after performing an action. This helps users understand the consequences of their actions and guides them in making more informed decisions or corrections in the future.
appliesWhen: When a user completes an action or transaction
When a user makes a selection or changes a setting
When a user submits information or data
do: Provide clear and immediate response after a user action
Ensure the feedback is relevant and understandable
Use visual cues or messages to indicate the result of the action
dont: Avoid delaying system response
Don't provide vague or ambiguous feedback
Avoid providing feedback that doesn't directly relate to the user's action

title: Feedforward
oneLiner: Give users a preview of the outcome before they commit to an action.
definition: Feedforward is a user experience principle that involves providing users with information about what will happen before they take an action. This enhances predictability, reduces uncertainty, and aids user decision-making by providing context.
appliesWhen: When users are about to make a significant commitment or decision, such as deleting an account or purchasing an item
When an action will lead to a significant change in the system state
When the result of an action is not inherently obvious or intuitive to the user
do: Provide clear information about the outcome of an action before it's taken
Use plain language to describe the effects of an action
Ensure that feedforward information is visible and easily understood
dont: Avoid using technical jargon that might confuse users
Don't hide feedforward information in tooltips or other non-obvious places
Do not overload the user with too much information

title: Fitts's Law
oneLiner: Larger, closer targets are faster to reach
definition: The time required to move to a target depends on the target's size and distance. Larger targets that are closer to the starting position can be acquired faster than smaller, more distant targets.
do: Make important buttons larger and easier to hit
Place frequently used controls closer to users' natural hand positions
Ensure touch targets are at least 44pt (iOS) or 48dp (Android)
Position related actions close together
dont: Make critical buttons too small or hard to reach
Place important actions at screen edges on large displays
Use tiny touch targets that require precision
Separate related actions by large distances

title: Flow State
oneLiner: Design for an immersive, engaging experience where users are fully absorbed in task completion.
definition: Flow State is an optimal user experience where individuals are so engaged in a task that they lose track of time and their surroundings. It's a balance between challenge and skill, where the task at hand is just difficult enough to maintain interest but not so hard that it becomes frustrating.
appliesWhen: Designing tasks that require deep concentration
Creating engaging games or interactive content
Building complex applications where users can progress and develop skills
do: Provide clear goals and immediate feedback
Ensure challenge matches user skill level
Minimize interruptions and distractions
dont: Overwhelm users with difficult tasks without appropriate skill level
Interrupt user flow with unnecessary notifications or prompts
Create boring or overly simple tasks that do not challenge the user

title: Hick's Law
oneLiner: More options → slower decisions
definition: The time it takes to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number of choices available. Users become overwhelmed and decision-making slows when presented with too many options.
do: Limit menu items to 5-7 main categories
Use progressive disclosure to reveal options gradually
Group similar options together logically
Highlight recommended or popular choices
dont: Present all options at once without organization
Create menus with more than 7-10 items
Force users to choose from dozens of similar options
Hide important actions too deeply

title: Internal Trigger
oneLiner: Leverage users' emotional states and memories to engage them with your product.
definition: An Internal Trigger is a UX principle that refers to the emotional states, memories, or personal experiences that prompt users to engage with a product or service. These triggers are often linked to deeply ingrained habits or behaviors, making them powerful tools for user engagement and retention.
appliesWhen: When designing app notifications or reminders
When designing a product or service that relies on user habit formation
When creating a marketing campaign that aims to resonate emotionally with potential users
do: Understand your users to identify potential internal triggers
Design with empathy, considering the emotional context in which your product will be used
Use subtle cues, such as notifications or reminders, to evoke these internal triggers
dont: Don't manipulate or exploit users' emotions or personal experiences
Don't rely solely on internal triggers; ensure your product also has external triggers
Don't overuse this principle as it can lead to user fatigue or annoyance

title: Jakob's Law
oneLiner: Users expect your site to work like other sites they know
definition: Users spend most of their time on other websites and mobile apps, so they prefer your site to work the same way as all the other sites they already know. Consistency with established patterns reduces cognitive load.
do: Follow established design patterns and conventions
Place navigation where users expect it (top or left)
Use familiar icons and interaction patterns
Match user expectations for common actions
dont: Reinvent common interface patterns unnecessarily
Place navigation in unexpected locations
Use unconventional icons without labels
Create unique flows for standard actions

title: Mental Model Principle
oneLiner: Users bring past experiences to new interfaces, influencing their expectations and interactions.
definition: The mental model principle suggests that users approach new interfaces with existing assumptions based on their past experiences and knowledge. These mental models impact how they perceive and interact with the interface, affecting their overall user experience.
appliesWhen: When designing a new interface for a familiar product or service
When introducing new features in an existing interface
When designing interfaces for complex systems
do: Understand your users' existing mental models to anticipate their expectations
Make the interface intuitive and easy to learn by aligning it with common mental models
Provide clear instructions and guidance to help users adjust their mental models if needed
dont: Ignore the influence of users' past experiences on their current expectations
Design without considering common mental models
Overload users with too much new information, disrupting their mental models

title: Nudging Principle
oneLiner: Influencing user behavior subtly to guide decision-making.
definition: Nudging is a UX principle where users can be subtly influenced into making certain decisions.
do: Use subtle visual cues
Guide towards beneficial choices
Maintain user autonomy
dont: Force specific actions
Use manipulative techniques
Override user preferences

title: Occam's Razor
oneLiner: The simplest solution is often the best.
definition: This principle recommends that when faced with multiple possible solutions, the simplest one that effectively solves the problem should be chosen. In the context of UX, it emphasizes clarity, simplicity, and minimalism in design decisions to enhance user experience.
do: Prioritize simplicity and clarity in design
Eliminate unnecessary elements
Focus on the user's needs and objectives
dont: Overcomplicate the user interface
Add unnecessary features
Ignore user feedback in favor of complex solutions

title: Pareto Principle
oneLiner: 80% of effects come from 20% of causes
definition: Also known as the 80/20 rule, this principle suggests that roughly 80% of outcomes result from 20% of inputs. In UX, 80% of users typically use only 20% of features, so focus design effort on those critical features.
do: Identify the 20% of features used by 80% of users
Make the most important features easily accessible
Prioritize optimization efforts on high-impact areas
Design primary actions prominently
dont: Give equal visual weight to all features
Bury commonly used functions in submenus
Spend equal development time on all features
Clutter interfaces with rarely used options

title: Provide Exit Points
oneLiner: Always give users a clear and easy way out
definition: This principle emphasizes the importance of providing users with easily identifiable exit options in your design. It ensures that users always feel in control of their actions and can leave or change course when they want to, reducing user anxiety and improving overall user experience.
appliesWhen: During the design of navigation menus
When creating forms or multi-step processes
When designing pop-up windows or modal dialogs
do: Always provide a clear and easy way to close a dialog or cancel an operation
Place exit options in intuitive locations
Ensure exit options are clearly distinguishable from other elements
dont: Do not trap users in certain sections of your site or app
Avoid making exit options hard to find or ambiguous
Do not force users to complete unnecessary steps before they can exit

title: Self-Initiated Triggers Principle
oneLiner: Empower users with self-controlled prompts, not system-generated ones.
definition: This principle suggests that prompts and reminders initiated by users are more effective than those generated by the system. User-controlled triggers cater to individual needs and preferences, enhancing engagement and task completion rates.
appliesWhen: When designing educational platforms or applications
do: Allow users to set their own reminders and notifications
Provide easy-to-use controls for managing prompts
Make it simple for users to adjust the frequency and timing of reminders
dont: Avoid bombarding users with unsolicited notifications
Don't force users to follow system-generated prompts without customization options
Do not make it difficult for users to turn off or adjust reminders

title: Skeuomorphism
oneLiner: Mimicking real-world objects in digital interfaces enhances user understanding
definition: Skeuomorphism is a design principle that involves creating digital interfaces that resemble their real-world counterparts. This approach helps users understand the functionality of digital elements by leveraging their pre-existing knowledge about physical objects.
appliesWhen: Designing new digital products for novice users
Designing for older demographics who may be unfamiliar with digital interfaces
do: Use skeuomorphic design when introducing new technologies to ease user understanding
Consider real-world objects and their behaviors when designing digital interfaces
Use skeuomorphism to create intuitive and user-friendly designs
dont: Avoid overuse of skeuomorphism as it can lead to cluttered and confusing interfaces
Don't use skeuomorphism when it reduces the efficiency of the interface
Avoid using skeuomorphism when designing for tech-savvy users who are familiar with abstract digital interfaces

title: Temptation Coupling
oneLiner: Difficult tasks become easier when paired with something pleasurable.
definition: Temptation Coupling is a principle suggesting that the likelihood of completing a difficult task increases when it's paired with an enjoyable activity. By linking something challenging with something rewarding, the overall experience becomes more positive, encouraging completion.
appliesWhen: Designing user onboarding or learning experiences
Motivating users to complete complex or tedious tasks
Encouraging long-term user engagement with the product
do: Identify the difficult tasks within your product and pair them with rewarding or enjoyable elements
Use gamification elements to make the task more engaging
Provide immediate rewards or feedback to the user after task completion
dont: Don’t make the rewarding element too distracting or overwhelming, it should not overshadow the primary task
Avoid making the task more complicated by adding unnecessary elements
Don't rely solely on rewards, the task itself should still hold some intrinsic value

title: Tesler's Law
oneLiner: Complexity can't be eliminated, only relocated within the system.
definition: Tesler's Law, also known as The Law of Conservation of Complexity, suggests that for every application, there is a certain amount of complexity that cannot be eliminated. Instead, this complexity can only be shifted around to different parts of the system.
appliesWhen: Designing for a diverse user base with various levels of expertise
do: Balance complexity between user interface and the system
Shift complexity to the parts of the system accessed by advanced users
Simplify user-facing components as much as possible
dont: Attempt to remove all complexity from the system
Make all parts of the system equally complex
Underestimate user ability to handle complexity if it’s presented intuitively

title: Weber's Law
oneLiner: Users find it easier to adapt to gradual changes rather than sudden, dramatic modifications in the interface.
definition: Weber's Law suggests that users are more comfortable with small, incremental changes in the design of a product or system. Dramatic changes, on the other hand, can disrupt the user's experience and lead to confusion or frustration.
appliesWhen: When updating or redesigning an existing product
When introducing new features or functionalities
When transitioning users from one platform to another
do: Implement changes incrementally
Test small changes with a subset of users before a full rollout
Provide clear and timely communication about upcoming changes
dont: Avoid large, dramatic interface changes all at once
