Don't remove familiar elements without proper explanation
Don't make changes without considering the impact on the user's experience

title: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
oneLiner: Designs that are aesthetically pleasing are perceived as more usable by users.
definition: The Aesthetic-Usability Effect refers to users' tendency to perceive attractive products as more usable. Even if two designs have identical functionality, the one that is more visually appealing will be perceived as easier to use.
appliesWhen: Designing the user interface for a product or service
Choosing the color scheme for a website or app
Creating the layout for a web page or app screen
do: Invest time in making the design visually appealing
Use aesthetically pleasing color combinations
Ensure the design is visually balanced and harmonious
dont: Neglect the visual aspect of your design
Assume that functionality alone will make a design usable
Underestimate the impact of aesthetics on user perception

title: Cheerleader Effect
oneLiner: Individual items appear more attractive when displayed alongside similar items.
definition: The Cheerleader Effect is a cognitive bias that suggests items displayed as a group are often perceived as more attractive than when displayed individually. This is because our brains process information collectively, creating an average composite that appears more appealing.
appliesWhen: When displaying a collection of similar items, such as product listings on an e-commerce site
When presenting a group of choices to the user
When arranging related images or content in a gallery format
do: Group related items together to enhance their perceived value
Use this effect to highlight a group of features or products rather than single ones
Consider using this principle when designing product or service packages
dont: Avoid grouping dissimilar or unrelated items together as it can diminish the effect
Don't overlook the individual attractiveness of items, as this effect doesn't replace the need for quality and appeal of individual items
Don't overuse this effect as it may lead to cognitive overload if too many items are grouped together

title: Contrast Principle
oneLiner: Use high contrast to draw user attention and emphasize key elements.
definition: The Contrast Principle refers to the use of distinct visual differences between elements to draw users' attention and create emphasis. High contrasting elements stand out, guiding the user’s eye to important information or actions.
appliesWhen: When you want to draw attention to a specific element
When you want to distinguish between different types of information or actions
When you want to guide user's eye through a sequence of actions
do: Use high contrast for primary action buttons or important information
Use contrast to visually group or separate different elements
Adjust contrast levels based on the importance of elements
dont: Don't use high contrast for all elements, as it can lead to visual clutter
Don't use high contrast for unimportant information or actions
Don't ignore accessibility standards while applying contrast

title: Law of Proximity
oneLiner: Objects close together are perceived as related
definition: Items that are spatially close to each other tend to be perceived as belonging to the same group. This Gestalt principle helps users understand relationships and organize information visually.
do: Group related form fields close together
Place labels near their corresponding inputs
Use consistent spacing to show relationships
Separate unrelated content with white space
dont: Place unrelated elements too close together
Use inconsistent spacing that confuses groupings
Separate labels too far from their inputs
Crowd the interface without logical grouping

title: Law of Prägnanz
oneLiner: Users simplify complex visuals to the most organized form possible
definition: The Law of Prägnanz states that when users face ambiguous or complex visual representations, they instinctively interpret them in the simplest, most complete, and organized way possible to reduce cognitive effort.
appliesWhen: When using abstract or symbolic representations
do: Simplify visual designs to reduce cognitive load
Use clear, straightforward representations
Organize information in a structured and easy-to-understand manner
dont: Overcomplicate visual designs
Present data in an unorganized manner
Use ambiguous or abstract symbols without clear explanations

title: Law of Similarity
oneLiner: Design elements that share visual characteristics are perceived to be more related by users.
definition: The Law of Similarity posits that users naturally group together and associate design elements that share similar visual characteristics, such as color, shape, size, or orientation. This principle is a fundamental aspect of Gestalt psychology, which emphasizes holistic processing.
appliesWhen: Designing UI elements that represent related functions or content
Organizing information or options in a layout
do: Use similar visual characteristics to group related UI elements or content
Leverage similarity to create or reinforce structure within your design
Utilize the law of similarity to guide users' attention and interactions
dont: Avoid making unrelated elements visually similar, which can lead to confusion
Do not overuse similarity to the point where it loses its effectiveness
Don't neglect the importance of contrast and differentiation within groups of similar elements

title: Picture Superiority Effect
oneLiner: Images are processed faster and remembered better than text.
definition: The Picture Superiority Effect refers to the phenomenon where pictures and images are more likely to be remembered than words. This is because visual stimuli are processed more quickly and easily by the brain, enhancing comprehension and memory recall.
do: Incorporate relevant images or graphics to complement text
Use visual cues to guide user attention and action
Consider the use of infographics for complex data or information
dont: Overwhelm users with too many visuals
Use irrelevant or ambiguous images
Rely solely on text for important information

title: Signifiers
oneLiner: Guide users with visual or textual cues to interact with interface elements effectively.
definition: Signifiers are intuitive symbols, text, or other visual elements that provide guidance on how to interact with different components of a user interface. They are essential in helping users understand how to navigate and use an application or website without explicit instruction.
appliesWhen: Adding new features to an existing interface
Improving user experience on a platform
do: Ensure signifiers are intuitive and easy to understand
Use established symbols or icons as signifiers for common actions
Place signifiers close to the element they are signifying
dont: Avoid overloading the interface with too many signifiers
Don't use abstract or unfamiliar symbols as signifiers
Don't place signifiers too far from the element they're related to

title: Visual Anchors
oneLiner: Use visual elements to guide user attention and enhance navigation.
definition: Visual anchors are design elements strategically placed to direct user attention and guide visual flow. They serve as reference points, helping users navigate an interface by drawing their eye to specific areas or information.
appliesWhen: When there's a lot of information to present
When guiding users through a process or journey
do: Use consistent visual anchors to enhance user predictability
Place anchors strategically to guide users through the interface
Use different shapes, sizes or colors to differentiate visual anchors
dont: Overload the interface with too many visual anchors
Place anchors randomly without considering their strategic importance
Use confusing or complex shapes as visual anchors

title: Visual Hierarchy
oneLiner: Design in a way that guides the eye, communicating the order and importance of information.
definition: Visual Hierarchy is a key principle in design that establishes an order to the visual elements on a page. It influences the order in which the human eye perceives what it sees, guiding the viewer's eye to certain elements in order of priority and importance.
appliesWhen: Designing a webpage or app interface
Creating a multi-step process or flow
do: Use size, color, and placement to establish hierarchy
Ensure that the most important elements are most prominent
Create clear distinctions between different levels in the hierarchy
dont: Avoid cluttering the design with too many competing elements
Don't neglect the balance and space in your design
Avoid inconsistency, which can confuse users about what's most important
